MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 9 – “P” for PricePart 4 - Price Changes and Public Policy and pricing.
Chapter 9 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

9.6 Discuss the key issues related to initiating and responding to price changes.
Learning Objectives for Lecture 9 for Part 3 (1 of 1)

Part 4  - Price Changes and Public Policy and pricing

Price competition is a core element of our free-market economy.
In setting prices, companies usually are not free to charge whatever prices they wish.
Many laws govern the rules of fair play in pricing.
Companies must also consider broader societal pricing concerns.
Marketers need to be familiar with the law and when uncertain as to what to do they must discuss the issue with legal experts (and NOT to put it in writing).
Public policy and pricing (1 of 4)

Figure 9.6
Public policy issues in pricing

Pricing within channel levels
Prohibited practices when pricing within channel levels include:
price fixing (i.e. sellers must set prices without talking to competitors)
predatory pricing (i.e. sellers cannot sell below cost with the intention of punishing a competitor or putting competitors out of business).
Public policy and pricing (3 of 4)
Q: Can you give some examples where price fixing, or predatory pricing has occurred?
A: In Australia, the cardboard carton manufacturers, were found to have colluded to fix artificially high prices. After this finding many of their large customers demanded that the carton manufacturers refund to them the excess amounts they had paid for cartons.

Pricing across channel levels
Prohibited practices when pricing across channel levels include:
price discrimination (i.e. offering different price or trading terms to different customers, except in certain circumstances).
retail (or resale) price maintenance (i.e. manufacturers requiring retailers to charge specific retail prices for their products).
deceptive pricing (i.e. stating or advertising prices that mislead consumer or are unavailable to the consumer).
Public policy and pricing (4 of 4)

Coming up next
That completes Lecture 9
Coming up  next is Lecture 10 - Branding
